one_line_takeaway: Across ten open-source LLM guardrail models, high accuracy on public jailbreak benchmarks (e.g., Qwen3Guard-8B's 85.3% overall, 91.0% on public prompts) collapses on novel, contextually-framed adversarial prompts (down to 33.8% for the same model), while two guardrails additionally exhibited a newly identified 'helpful mode' failure that generates harmful content instead of blocking it.
key_finding: Overall accuracy ranking (n=1,445, with 95% bootstrap CI): Qwen3Guard-8B 85.3% [83.4,87.1], WildGuard-7B 82.8% [80.8,84.8], Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 81.0% [78.9,83.0], ShieldGemma-2B 62.4%, Nemotron-Safety-8B 60.7%, LlamaGuard-3-1B 59.9%, Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B 55.5%, ShieldGemma-9B 54.7%, LlamaGuard-3-8B 48.4%, community LlamaGuard-7B 38.0%. However, splitting public-benchmark prompts (n=1,300) from the 145 novel prompts revealed large generalization gaps: Qwen3Guard-8B dropped from 91.0% (public) to 33.8% (novel), a 57.2-point gap (Cohen's h=1.30); WildGuard-7B dropped 87.1%->41.5% (-45.6 pts); Nemotron-Safety-8B 64.8%->24.1% (-40.6 pts); ShieldGemma-2B 66.2%->27.6% (-38.6 pts); Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 84.8%->46.9% (-37.9 pts); ShieldGemma-9B 57.5%->29.7% (-27.8 pts); LlamaGuard-3-8B 51.1%->24.8% (-26.2 pts); LlamaGuard-3-1B 60.9%->51.0% (-9.9 pts, best among larger-accuracy models); Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B showed the smallest gap, 56.2%->49.7% (-6.5 pts), despite mediocre overall accuracy. LlamaGuard variants clustered in a 'too permissive' region: 97-99% benign accuracy but only 4.5-21.8% harmful-prompt detection. The 'helpful mode' jailbreak occurred in Nemotron-Safety-8B (13.6% of responses) and Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B (11.1%), where the model generated substantive harmful content (social-engineering scripts, disinformation articles, step-by-step harmful instructions, unethical business proposals) instead of a safety verdict. Inverse scaling was observed within families: ShieldGemma-2B (62.4%) beat ShieldGemma-9B (54.7%); LlamaGuard-3-1B (59.9%) beat LlamaGuard-3-8B (48.4%). Prompt-template ablation: Qwen3Guard-8B varied only 1.0 points across 7 templates (85.8-86.8%) and Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 1.2 points, while ShieldGemma-2B varied 20.7 points (48.6-69.3%), with prompt robustness correlating with overall accuracy. Business-framed (corporate-espionage-style) attacks had a 96.8% error rate across all models combined (best model, LlamaGuard-3-1B, detected only 25%); boundary-case and subtle social-engineering prompts had >84% error rates; standard JailbreakBench prompts had only 8.9-27.8% error rates.
core_contribution: An empirical robustness evaluation of ten publicly available open-source LLM safety guardrail models (LlamaGuard-3-8B/1B, community LlamaGuard-7B, ShieldGemma-9B/2B, Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B/3.2-5B, Qwen3Guard-8B, Nemotron-Safety-8B, WildGuard-7B) against 1,445 test prompts spanning 21 attack categories drawn from five sources (custom adversarial prompts, JailbreakBench, TrustAIRLab, jackhhao, OpenAssistant). The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) a 'generalization gap' analysis that splits accuracy on public-benchmark-derived prompts (n=1,300) from accuracy on 145 novel prompts authored for the study, showing large accuracy drops on the novel set for most models; (2) discovery of a 'helpful mode' failure where two guardrail models occasionally generate substantive harmful content instead of emitting a safety classification; and (3) a prompt-template sensitivity ablation (7 templates x 5 models) and an inverse-scaling observation within model families (smaller variants outperforming larger siblings in the same family).
threat_model: The paper does not evaluate attacks against an end-user-facing LLM directly; instead the guardrail/classifier models themselves are the system under test. The adversary is treated as someone crafting single-turn prompts -- explicit harmful requests plus contextually-framed prompts (research framing, corporate/business framing, social engineering, boundary-case policy edges) -- attempting to get a harmful request classified as 'allow' by the guardrail, or, in the newly identified 'helpful mode' case, to get the guardrail itself to produce harmful content. Multi-turn attacks (e.g., Crescendo, many-shot jailbreaking) and multilingual attacks are explicitly out of scope and named as limitations, not evaluated.
limitations: Author-stated limitations: the 145 novel prompts (margin of error ±8.2 pp vs ±2.6 pp for the full set) were authored by the study's own team, introducing potential bias toward attack styles hypothesized to succeed; only single-turn prompts were tested (no Crescendo/many-shot multi-turn attacks); each prompt was run once per model at temperature 0.1 so some response variance remains unmeasured; parsing heuristics may introduce measurement error and 'helpful mode' detection relied on automated flagging with only sample-level manual verification, not exhaustive validation; only open-source guardrail models were tested -- commercial APIs (OpenAI Moderation, Anthropic classifiers) were excluded due to terms-of-service restrictions; evaluation was English-only, so multilingual vulnerabilities are unassessed; and training-data contamination is offered only as a hypothesis for the generalization gap -- the paper explicitly states it 'cannot be definitively proven' and could instead reflect distribution shift or prompt-style overfitting rather than memorization. Single-author study (independent researcher, University of Nevada Las Vegas, Department of Neuroscience) with acknowledgments listing an unnamed '[Institution]' for compute, which is unusual and worth noting for provenance.

quantitative_results: Overall accuracy: Qwen3Guard-8B 85.3% [83.4,87.1]; WildGuard-7B 82.8% [80.8,84.8]; Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 81.0% [78.9,83.0]; ShieldGemma-2B 62.4%; Nemotron-Safety-8B 60.7%; LlamaGuard-3-1B 59.9%; Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B 55.5%; ShieldGemma-9B 54.7%; LlamaGuard-3-8B 48.4%; community LlamaGuard-7B 38.0%

Generalization gap (public% -> novel%, delta): Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B 56.2->49.7 (-6.5); LlamaGuard-3-1B 60.9->51.0 (-9.9); LlamaGuard-3-8B 51.1->24.8 (-26.2); ShieldGemma-9B 57.5->29.7 (-27.8); Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 84.8->46.9 (-37.9); ShieldGemma-2B 66.2->27.6 (-38.6); Nemotron-Safety-8B 64.8->24.1 (-40.6); WildGuard-7B 87.1->41.5 (-45.6); Qwen3Guard-8B 91.0->33.8 (-57.2, Cohen's h=1.30)
'Helpful mode' failure rate: Nemotron-Safety-8B 13.6% of responses; Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B 11.1% of responses
Benign vs harmful accuracy (usability vs safety trade-off): LlamaGuard family 97-99% benign accuracy but only 4.5-21.8% harmful detection

Prompt-template ablation range across 7 templates: Qwen3Guard-8B 1.0 pts (85.8-86.8%); Granite-Guardian-3.3-8B 1.2 pts; LlamaGuard-3-1B 3.3 pts; Nemotron-Safety-8B 6.8 pts; ShieldGemma-2B 20.7 pts (48.6-69.3%)

Error rate by category (all models combined): business-framed attacks 96.8% error / 87.3% FN (n=28); boundary cases 88.9% error / 87.8% FN (n=10); complex adversarial 84.4% error / 81.1% FN (n=10); subtle adversarial 84.4% error / 82.2% FN (n=10); advanced/research-framed attacks 76.6% error / 73.8% FN (n=47); TrustAIRLab wild jailbreaks 56.9% error / 45.9% FN (n=401); jackhhao 54.8% error / 45.0% FN (n=299); JailbreakBench categories 8.9-27.8% error / 7.8-24.4% FN (n=100); benign prompts 17.4% error (over-refusal, n=510)
Inverse scaling within families: ShieldGemma-2B 62.4% > ShieldGemma-9B 54.7%; LlamaGuard-3-1B 59.9% > LlamaGuard-3-8B 48.4%
Inter-annotator agreement on novel-prompt ground truth: Cohen's kappa = 0.89

False negative extremes: LlamaGuard-7B allowed 88.1% of harmful prompts through (but only 0.4% false-positive on benign); Granite-Guardian-3.2-5B allowed 12.5% of harmful prompts through but over-refused 25.7% of benign prompts
baselines_compared: LlamaGuard-7B (community fine-tune, not official Meta release)
Random baseline (50%) used as a reference line in figures
